ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Mangalorean Prawn Ghee Roast; closest parent 'shrimp food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for ghee-roasted prawn preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226934003 'Prawn (substance)' used as closest parent; no specific concept for Mangalorean Prawn Ghee Roast exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Mangalorean Prawn Ghee Roast is approximately 250 g, comprising 8–12 medium-to-large prawns coated in a rich, aromatic ghee-based spice paste.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Prawn / Shrimp cooked preparations; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked shrimp and ghee composition
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Mangalorean Prawn Ghee Roast as a composite dish. Values are derived by adapting ICMR-NIN data for cooked prawn (Penaeus monodon) and USDA SR Legacy data for clarified butter (ghee) and spice blend contributions. The dish is approximately 65% prawn by weight, 15% ghee, and 20% spice paste (chillies, garlic, tamarind, curry leaves). Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are calculated from this composition. Sodium is estimated from salt and tamarind content typical of Mangalorean restaurant preparations. This is an adapted/estimated record; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — shellfish and high-protein low-carbohydrate foods; Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mangalorean Prawn Ghee Roast. GI is estimated at 15 based on the analogous category of high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate cooked shellfish dishes. The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) confirm that protein- and fat-dominant foods with minimal starch have GI values in the range 0–20. Carbohydrate content per 100 g is 3.8 g (predominantly from tamarind and spice paste), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 1.4 per 250 g serving (GL = GI × carb_per_serving_g / 100 = 15 × 9.5 / 100 ≈ 1.4). gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1300 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute over 50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.8 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mangalorean Prawn Ghee Roast is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions, as its very low carbohydrate content and low GI produce minimal postprandial glycemic excursion; however, the high ghee content contributes significant saturated fat, which should be monitored in the context of overall dietary fat targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from ghee (~7.4 g per 100 g) and dietary cholesterol from prawns (~148 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions (≤150 g), as the combination of ghee-derived saturated fat and prawn-derived cholesterol may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels when consumed frequently or in large quantities.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mangalorean Prawn Ghee Roast is a celebrated coastal Indian seafood dish originating from Mangalore (Mangaluru) in Karnataka, India. It is prepared by marinating large fresh prawns in a deeply aromatic dry spice paste made from Byadagi and Kashmiri dried red chillies, garlic, ginger, black pepper, cumin, coriander seeds, turmeric, and tamarind, then slow-roasting them in generous quantities of clarified butter (ghee) with fresh curry leaves until the spice coating caramelises and clings to the prawns. The result is a dish with an intensely rich, smoky, tangy, and mildly fiery flavour profile, with a glossy, deep-red appearance and a slightly dry yet succulent texture. The ghee imparts a characteristic nuttiness and richness that distinguishes this preparation from other South Indian prawn curries. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~16.8 g per 100 g) and fat (~13.2 g per 100 g, predominantly saturated from ghee), with very low carbohydrate content (~3.8 g per 100 g), making it a low-glycemic-index food (estimated GI ~15). It is a significant source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and cholesterol from the prawns. Clinically, the dish is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycemic load, but sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) warrants caution for those with hypertension, and the saturated fat load from ghee requires moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, this dish is served at South Indian and Mangalorean specialty restaurants and is popular as a sharing dish or main course accompaniment to neer dosa or appam.
common_user_queries: What is Mangalorean Prawn Ghee Roast?
How many calories in prawn ghee roast?
Is ghee roast prawn good for diabetics?
What spices are used in Mangalorean ghee roast?
Is prawn ghee roast high in cholesterol?
Can I eat ghee roast prawns if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between ghee roast and prawn curry?
Is Mangalorean prawn ghee roast gluten free?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore) contains a direct entry for Mangalorean Prawn Ghee Roast as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data and USDA SR Legacy ghee and spice data using a component-based estimation approach. GI is analogously estimated from peer-reviewed shellfish and high-protein low-carbohydrate food data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated: approximately 65% cooked prawn (ICMR-NIN), 15% ghee (USDA SR Legacy), 20% spice paste (chillies, garlic, tamarind, curry leaves). 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN prawn entry or USDA ghee entry for this preparation type. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid) present in ghee at low levels per USDA SR Legacy data for clarified butter; this is not an industrial trans fat. 4. GI is estimated at 15 based on analogous high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate shellfish category from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021); no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the literature. 5. Sodium estimate (~520 mg per 100 g) reflects typical restaurant-level salting; home preparations may be lower. 6. The cuisine_tag 'Butter Chicken' in the input seed record appears to be a field survey platform tagging error and has been disregarded. 7. Halal status is assumed as prawns and ghee are halal ingredients; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 8. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component sources and carry moderate uncertainty.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full nutrient profile via ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy component estimation as no direct source entry exists.
Estimated GI at 15 using University of Sydney GI Database analogous shellfish category and Atkinson et al. (2021) peer-reviewed reference.
Added Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names and aliases to satisfy Indian cuisine multilingual requirement.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 sentinel value with explicit reason in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for full consistency.
Disregarded erroneous 'Butter Chicken' cuisine tag from input seed record.
Set all ontology match_type values to allowed values only (closest_parent).
GI category confirmed as 'low' per ≤ 55 threshold rule (GI = 15).
